title: AI firm Anthropic's 'dangerous' Mythos model fuelling government's desire to lure company to Australia
full_text:
A new generation of AI models deemed "too dangerous" for public release is driving Australia's efforts to lure top AI companies to establish a major presence in the country.
The chief lawyer of AI company Anthropic, Jeffrey Bleich, flew into Australia this week for meetings with senior government officials.
On the agenda for the meetings is obtaining access to Anthropic's newest model, Claude Mythos.
Mythos and a similar new model from rival OpenAI are being touted as so capable at finding cybersecurity weaknesses that the companies fear major disruptions if the technology falls into the wrong hands.
Now, Australian agencies and companies are pushing for access to the models, adding to the buzzy AI company's leverage as it considers where it will set up major hubs outside the US.
A federal government briefing, obtained under freedom of information laws, shows Australia wants "major investments in Australia" and is willing to discuss reviewing copyright laws as part of a deal.
Anthropic briefs government, critical infrastructure on new AI cyber risks
Mr Bleich, who served as Barack Obama's ambassador to Australia from 2009 to 2013 and is now Anthropic's general counsel, met with Assistant Minister for Science, Technology and the Digital Economy, Andrew Charlton, staff from the Department of Home Affairs and the Australian Signals Directorate.
A major change since the company's US executives visited in April is the limited release of Mythos.
While AI makers often hype the capabilities of their products, organisations such as UK's AI Safety Institute and Mozilla, maker of web browser Firefox, have tested and vouch for the advanced capabilities of Mythos when it comes to finding bugs that could be exploited for cyber attacks.
Previously, Anthropic had released its models to any paying subscriber. But this time, the company chose to limit access to Mythos to about 50 mostly US-based technology companies such as Amazon, Microsoft, Apple and Google.
No Australian companies or organisations were on the list.
OpenAI has followed suit and is only releasing its newest cybersecurity-based model, GPT-5.5-Cyber, to select customers.
The federal government declined to confirm or deny that its agencies and Australian companies still do not have access to Mythos.
A spokesperson for the Department of Home Affairs said it had "extensive discussions with Anthropic on the cyber security implications of AI and improved cyber defence mechanisms".
Home Affairs has co-ordinated multiple meetings between Anthropic and Australian operators of critical infrastructure since the release of Mythos.
Late last month Anthropic held a briefing with 170 representatives from Australian finance, communications, transport, energy, data, food and grocery sectors.
These organisations are declared as Systems of National Significance, a subset of critical infrastructure providers considered crucial to national security.
A source with knowledge of the closed-door meetings between the government and Anthropic but not authorised to speak publicly said that Australian access to Mythos was a topic of discussion.
A spokesperson for OpenAI told the ABC it was starting to roll out its Mythos-like model, GPT-5.5-Cyber, to a limited number of Australian organisations.
"We have begun rollout of our Trusted Access for Cyber (TAC) program, including GPT-5.5-Cyber, to a handful of customers in Australia," they said. This includes Commonwealth Bank and Westpac, the Australian Financial Review reported.
A spokesperson for Home Affairs minister Tony Burke said that the government was working with the private sector to prepare for developments in AI.
"Our government has put in place systems to help our country adapt to and resist evolving cyber threats," they said.
Anthropic's spokesperson declined to comment on discussions in its meetings with government.
Australia's attempts to lure AI companies
In December the federal government released a national AI plan that included attracting big tech companies to invest here and mitigating risks from the technology.
AI companies such as Anthropic and OpenAI, as well as more established players such as Google, Meta and Amazon, see Australia as an attractive destination for building AI infrastructure in the southern hemisphere. Australia's skilled workforce, energy grid and political stability are all drawcards.
In the United States, Anthropic faces immense pressure from the Trump administration over its decision to limit the use of its models by the US military. The company is reportedly considering major expansion overseas.
The federal government wants Anthropic to pick Australia. It stepped up its courtship with a meeting on the sidelines of an international AI summit in India between Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei and Charlton in February this year.
Department of Industry briefing notes obtained through a freedom of information request reveal the government's thinking ahead of the meeting.
Under the heading "What we want", the document has three asks:
* That Anthropic make "major investments in ways that create benefits for Australia, including being additive to the energy grid"
* That it match commitments it made in the United States to cover grid infrastructure costs, build new power generation, reduce strain on the grid and invest in local communities
* That it commit to working with Australia on frontier AI safety and security
It also seeks more information on Anthropic's major hurdles: "[We want to] understand Anthropic's issues with Australia's copyright regime, how it affects their business and what alternatives have been explored."
A spokesperson for the department of industry declined to comment on the briefing document.
Groups representing tech companies and the private sector are arguing that Australia's copyright law limits AI investment in Australia.
So far, the Albanese government has ruled out giving AI companies an "text and data mining" exemption to train AI on Australian copyright material without payment.
Creatives fear the government will agree to water down protections to clear way for investment for AI companies.
Since the meeting in India the federal government and Anthropic have sought to formalise this increasingly close relationship.
Six weeks later, Industry Minister Tim Ayres met with Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei in Australia and signed a memorandum of understanding outlining a "shared vision" with the company.
The memo contains several of the asks from the briefing in non-binding forms.
The company also opened its Sydney office last month, its fourth office in the Asia-Pacific.
'Winners are being picked'
CyberCX chief strategy officer and former Australian national cybersecurity advisor Alastair MacGibbon said that securing access to cutting edge AI tools for Australian firms and agencies isn't the be-all-and-end-all.
A lot of Australia's digital infrastructure is built on systems created by major technology firms and big international financial institutions that have been given early access to Anthropic's new model, he said.
On the other hand, many organisations have their own bespoke software that will not be supported by companies that have had access to these AI models, Mr MacGibbon said.
"Winners are being picked, and [the tech companies] chose in a US-centric way who the winners were," he said.
Mr MacGibbon said the responsibility for closing that gap should fall on governments, not on individual companies trying to negotiate access on their own.
An Anthropic spokesperson declined to say when Australian organisations outside the existing partners will get access to Mythos.
"We've been having constructive conversations with government, financial institutions, and enterprise customers globally; and plan to expand access safely in coordination with them in due course," the spokesperson said.
summary: Australia is trying to attract AI company Anthropic to set up operations in the country, especially after its new AI model, Claude Mythos, was deemed too risky for public release. The government is willing to discuss changes to copyright laws to secure major investments from Anthropic.
author: national AI Cameron Wilson
heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Anthropic's general counsel, Jeffrey Bleich, met with Australian officials.
Australia seeks access to Anthropic's Claude Mythos model for cybersecurity.
The government is open to reviewing copyright laws to attract AI companies.
Anthropic has limited access to Mythos to about 50 US-based firms.
OpenAI is also limiting access to its new cybersecurity model in Australia.
Australia's national AI plan aims to draw big tech investments.
ticker_line: Australia courts Anthropic for AI investment
